topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an irritating, high-pitched whine that seemed to vibrate directly behind my eyes. I stared at the confirmation email, the words 'Congratulations, you have been selected for an interview,' mocking me. Across the small, cluttered desk, Liam was already grinning, a wide, almost unnerving display of satisfaction. I gripped the edge of the laminate surface until my knuckles turned white. Every muscle in my jaw felt tight, a constant, low-grade tension. *He got it. I didn't.* I reread the job description one last time, tracing the bullet points with a shaky finger, searching for some hidden flaw, some oversight I could exploit. My breath hitched, shallow and quick, as I shoved the laptop shut with unnecessary force. The silence in my tiny apartment felt cavernous, amplifying the frantic thumping in my chest.

[story 2]

My palms were slick against the cheap plastic of the application form. I kept wiping them on my jeans, a nervous, repetitive motion. When I finally looked up, Liam was leaning back in his chair, a slow, deliberate exhale escaping him. His casual confidence was a physical blow. I could feel the heat rising in my neck, a flush that had nothing to do with the room temperature. "Well?" he asked, his voice smooth, entirely devoid of the internal struggle I was currently battling. I managed a weak, strained smile that felt like cracking plaster. "It's... great news for you." The words tasted like ash. I wanted to argue, to point out the glaring discrepancy in my qualifications, but the energy just drained out of me, leaving only a heavy, leaden feeling in my limbs.

[story 3]

The clock on the wall ticked with agonizing slowness, each second a tiny hammer blow against my composure. I kept glancing at my phone, half-expecting a notification that would either confirm my failure or offer some miraculous reprieve. My throat felt constricted, dry and tight, making swallowing a conscious, difficult effort. I paced the small confines of the hallway, the worn carpet muffling my footsteps, each step fueled by a raw, unspent urgency. When Liam finally appeared, he didn't even look up from his tablet. "Still waiting on an update?" he inquired, his tone suggesting I was an inconvenience rather than a peer. I stopped abruptly, my shoulders slumping forward as if gravity had suddenly increased tenfold. "I... I just wanted to make sure everything was processed correctly," I mumbled, the sound barely a whisper, my gaze fixed firmly on the scuffed toes of my shoes.
